item.title: Making BUSCO pretty
item.description: What is BUSCO? Benchmarking sets of Universal Single-Copy Orthologs (or BUSCO) is a common method used to estimate the completeness and contamintation of a genome/transcriptome/proteome by counting a set number of genes that should only appear once in your dataset. See the user guide to give it a go yourself. The pipeline produces a pretty decent plot but let’s make our own … Setting up RStudio I typically work in RStudio, particularly for data visualisation.

item.title: Plotting phylogenies in R
item.description: # Load required libraries library(tidyverse) library(ape) library(ggtree) library(ggtreeExtra) library(ggnewscale) library(gginnards) library(treeio) # Define color palette for BUSCO status pal.BUSCO <- c(Complete = "#36AE7C", Duplicated = "#187498", Fragmented = "#F9D923", Missing = "#EB5353") # Read in tree file. Use read.iqtree for .contree files. # Alternatively, use path to treefile tree <- read.tree(text='((A, B)100, ((C, D)89, (E, F)90)70)100;') # Or use read.iqtree from the treeio package for .contree files using the path to the file # Create ggtree object of the tree and add tip labels and node labels ggtree(tree)+ geom_tiplab()+ # Add tip labels geom_nodelab() # Add node labels # Read in metadata for BUSCO analysis meta.
